paper_title: Identification and Estimation of Consumers' Preferences from Repeated Observations under Nonlinear Pricing\thanks\(^\dagger\)Bates White LLC and Toulouse School of Economics.\newline \(^\ddagger\)Toulouse School of Economics, University of Toulouse Capitole.\newline Authors wish to thank Ivana Komunjer, David Martimort, Stefan Pollinger, participants to the 2025 DMV Econometrics Workshop for their useful comments and remarks. Frédérique Fève and Jean-Pierre Florens acknowledge funding from the French National Research Agency (ANR) under the Investments for the Future program (Investissements d'Avenir, grant ANR-17-EURE-0010).

perturbations.original:
We make several contributions to the literature on consumer demand
estimation with nonlinear budget sets. First, we provide a novel
identification strategy that relies on multiple price schedules but
eschews parametric assumptions about preferences or their distribution.
Second, we frame the identification problem as an iterative functional
equation and develop a closed-form solution (see Kuczma, Choczewski, and
Ger (1990)). Third, we propose a fully nonparametric estimator, analyze
its theoretical properties, and propose a valid bootstrap procedure for
finite-sample inference. Fourth, we extend our framework to accommodate
endogenous prices and additional covariates, making it applicable to a
wide range of empirical settings.
perturbations.perturbed: We make several contributions to the literature on consumer demand estimation with nonlinear budget sets. First, we provide a novel identification strategy that relies on a single price schedule and eschews parametric assumptions about preferences or their distribution. Second, we frame the identification problem as an iterative functional equation and develop a closed-form solution (see Kuczma, Choczewski, and Ger (1990)). Third, we propose a fully nonparametric estimator, analyze its theoretical properties, and propose a valid bootstrap procedure for finite-sample inference. Fourth, we extend our framework to accommodate endogenous prices and additional covariates, making it applicable to a wide range of empirical settings.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text later states in Section 1 (S0013) and Section 2 that the identification results are established 'using two price schedules'. Claiming identification with a single price schedule contradicts the paper's core identification strategy.

perturbations.original:
We extend our framework to accommodate settings where prices are
potentially endogenous and need to be estimated from data. This involves
an instrumental variable approach, where consumer characteristics that
affect utility but not the price schedule serve as instruments. We also
extend the model to incorporate additional observed heterogeneity,
allowing the utility function to depend on consumer characteristics.
perturbations.perturbed: We extend our framework to accommodate settings where prices are potentially endogenous and need to be estimated from data. This involves an instrumental variable approach, where consumer characteristics that affect the price schedule but not the utility serve as instruments. We also extend the model to incorporate additional observed heterogeneity, allowing the utility function to depend on consumer characteristics.
perturbations.why_wrong: Standard IV logic for demand requires instruments that affect the price (supply side) but are excluded from the utility (demand side). The perturbation reverses this, claiming instruments affect utility but not price, which would violate the exclusion restriction for identifying demand.

perturbations.original:
Our methodology provides a flexible framework for analyzing consumer
behavior under nonlinear pricing that relies on minimal assumptions
about functional forms. This approach is particularly valuable in
contexts where imposing parametric restrictions is undesirable due to
concerns about misspecification. The identification strategy leverages
variation in price schedules, making it applicable in many empirical
settings where different price schedules are observed (e.g., different
markets, time periods, or regulatory regimes).
perturbations.perturbed: Our methodology provides a flexible framework for analyzing consumer behavior under nonlinear pricing that relies on minimal assumptions about functional forms. This approach is particularly valuable in contexts where imposing parametric restrictions is undesirable due to concerns about misspecification. The identification strategy leverages variation in the Lagrange multiplier $\tau$ across individuals, making it applicable in many empirical settings where different price schedules are observed.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper states that identification leverages variation in 'price schedules' (S0012 original) and that $\tau$ is an unknown parameter to be estimated (S0019), not the source of identification variation.

perturbations.original:
The remainder of the paper is organized as follows.
Section~\ref{sec-model} introduces the model and
Section~\ref{sec-identification} establishes the identification results
using two price schedules. Section~\ref{sec-estimation} develops the
estimation procedure and establishes its statistical properties.
Section~\ref{sec-bootstrap} presents a bootstrap approach for inference,
Section~\ref{sec-addexo} extends the framework to incorporate additional
observed heterogeneity, and Section~\ref{sec-priceest} addresses the
case of endogenous prices. Section~\ref{sec-montecarlo} contains a study
of the small-sample properties of our estimator. Finally,
Section~\ref{sec-application} presents an empirical application to
unaddressed advertising in a European country, where, among other
things, we discuss counterfactual elasticities to nonlinear prices.
perturbations.perturbed: The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section~\ref{sec-model} introduces the model and Section~\ref{sec-identification} establishes the identification results using a single price schedule. Section~\ref{sec-estimation} develops the estimation procedure and establishes its statistical properties. Section~\ref{sec-bootstrap} presents a bootstrap approach for inference, Section~\ref{sec-addexo} extends the framework to incorporate additional observed heterogeneity, and Section~\ref{sec-priceest} addresses the case of endogenous prices. Section~\ref{sec-montecarlo} contains a study of the small-sample properties of our estimator. Finally, Section~\ref{sec-application} presents an empirical application to unaddressed advertising in a European country, where, among other things, we discuss counterfactual elasticities to nonlinear prices.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper explicitly states in the original S0013 and subsequent sections that identification is established 'using two price schedules'.

perturbations.original:
We have individuals who consume a quantity \(Q\) of a good for a price
\(P(Q)\), which is not necessarily linear in \(Q\). Every individual has
a utility function that can be written as \(u(Q) + Q \varepsilon\),
where \(\varepsilon\) is an unobserved individual type generated by a
probability distribution on \(\R\), such that
\(\varepsilon \sim F_\varepsilon\).
perturbations.perturbed: We have individuals who consume a quantity \(Q\) of a good for a price \(P(Q)\), which is not necessarily linear in \(Q\). Every individual has a utility function that can be written as \(u(Q) + \varepsilon\), where \(\varepsilon\) is an unobserved individual type generated by a probability distribution on \(\R\), such that \(\varepsilon \sim F_\varepsilon\).
perturbations.why_wrong: The first-order condition in Equation 1 (S0016) is derived from a utility function of the form $u(Q) + Q\varepsilon$. If utility were $u(Q) + \varepsilon$, the derivative with respect to $Q$ would not contain $\varepsilon$, making the FOC $\nu'(Q) - \tau P'(Q) = 0$, which contradicts the provided Equation 1.

The second-order condition for a maximization problem requires the second derivative to be negative. The perturbation sets $u'' - \tau P'' > 0$, which would imply a minimum rather than a maximum, contradicting the 'maximization problem' described in S0016.

perturbations.perturbed:
Every individual solves the following maximization problem
\[\max_{Q} u(Q) + Q \varepsilon - \tau( P(Q)- B),\] where \(\tau > 0\)
is a Lagrange multiplier and \(B\) is the total budget available to
purchase the good. As \(B\) is not observed, we assume that the
constraint is never binding, and therefore \(\tau\) is strictly
positive. For a given \(\varepsilon\), the optimal quantity demanded,
\(Q^\ast\), satisfies the following first order condition
\begin{equation}\phantomsection\label{eq-eq:optquant}{\nu^\prime(\bar{Q}) - \tau P^\prime(\bar{Q}) = -\varepsilon, \text{ with } Q^\ast = \bar{Q} \vee 0. 
}\end{equation}
perturbations.why_wrong: If the budget constraint is 'never binding', the Lagrange multiplier $\tau$ would be zero by complementary slackness. The text's claim that $\tau$ is strictly positive requires the constraint to be binding.

perturbations.original:
Under the conditions in Assumption \ref{ass:regcond}(i), the
maximization problem in Equation~\ref{eq-eq:optquant} has a unique
solution. Assumptions \ref{ass:regcond}(ii)-(iii) are regularity
conditions on the support and cdf of \(\varepsilon\).
perturbations.perturbed: Under the conditions in Assumption \ref{ass:regcond}(i), the maximization problem in Equation~\ref{eq-eq:optquant} has multiple solutions. Assumptions \ref{ass:regcond}(ii)-(iii) are regularity conditions on the support and cdf of \(\varepsilon\).
perturbations.why_wrong: Assumption 1(i) (S0015) specifies that the objective function is strictly concave ($u'' - \tau P'' < 0$). A strictly concave function on a convex set has a unique maximum, not multiple solutions.

perturbations.original:
We let \(\varphi(Q) = u^\prime(Q) - \tau P^\prime(Q)\) which, from
Assumption \ref{ass:regcond}, is a strictly decreasing function defined
on \(\R^{+}\).
perturbations.perturbed: We let \(\varphi(Q) = u^\prime(Q) - \tau P^\prime(Q)\) which, from Assumption \ref{ass:regcond}, is a strictly increasing function defined on \(\R^{+}\).
perturbations.why_wrong: Assumption 1(i) states $u'' - \tau P'' < 0$. Since $\varphi'(Q) = u''(Q) - \tau P''(Q)$, the function $\varphi(Q)$ must be strictly decreasing, not increasing.

perturbations.original:
The price schedule \(P(Q)\) is supposed to be known. Hence, the unknown
parameters of this model are the utility function \(u(Q)\), the
distribution of types \(F_\varepsilon\), and the Lagrange multiplier
\(\tau\).
perturbations.perturbed: The price schedule \(P(Q)\) is supposed to be known. Hence, the unknown parameters of this model are the utility function \(u(Q)\) and the distribution of types \(F_\varepsilon\), while the Lagrange multiplier \(\tau\) is a known constant.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text and the logic of the model treat $\tau$ as an unknown parameter to be identified/estimated. If $\tau$ were known, the identification problem would be significantly different from the iterative functional equation approach described in S0010.
